content.problemStatement:
Chef loves palindromes!

A palindrome is a string that reads the same forward and backward. For example, "racecar"\texttt{"racecar"}"racecar" and "aibohphobia"\texttt{"aibohphobia"}"aibohphobia" are examples of palindromes, while "fly"\texttt{"fly"}"fly" and "palindrome"\texttt{"palindrome"}"palindrome" are not.

Chef has with him a string SSS of length NNN, containing only lowercase English letters.

He wants to turn SSS into a palindrome. To do that, he can perform the following operation:


Choose a substring of SSS that's not a palindrome and doesn't equal the full string, and delete this substring from SSS. The remaining characters don't change their order.
Is it possible for Chef to turn SSS into a palindrome by using several (possibly, zero) of these operations?

If it is, also find the minimum number of operations necessary.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the length of the string SSS.
The second line contains a string SSS of length NNN, containing only lowercase English letters.




Output Format
For each test case, print the answer on a new line:



If it's not possible to turn SSS into a palindrome no matter what, print the integer −1-1−1.
Otherwise, print the minimum number of operations Chef needs.
constraints: SiS_iSi​ is one of the characters `'a', 'b', 'c', ..., 'z'`.
The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052\cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: The string is already a palindrome, no operations are necessary.
Test case 222: The string is not a palindrome, but Chef also has no valid moves - there's no substring that's both not a palindrome and also not equal to the full string.
So, it's impossible to change the string, and the answer is −1-1−1.
Test case 333: Chef can delete the substring od\texttt{od}od comprising indices 222 and 333.
This turns the string into "mem"\texttt{"mem"}"mem", which is a palindrome.
